coredata.dc:title: A ratio fluorescence probe by one-stage process for selectivity detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Sen
Liu, Jiaxin
Luo, Junjie
Wang, Shuo
Li, Yu
Li, Qingbi
Chen, Shuangshuang
Wang, Guoping
coredata.dc:description: With the rapid development of society, drug residues in people's living environments are becoming increasingly serious. When the drug residues in the human body exceed a certain standard, it will have a number of adverse effects on human health. Therefore, a ratio fluorescent probe (Eu-CDs) was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method for the selectivity detection of tetracycline. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (I623/I473) and tetracycline concentration demonstrated a good linear relationship that ranges from 0.1 to 80 μM with a low detection limit of 0.057 μM in DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide). Similarly, the fluorescent probe presents good selectivity as well as anti-interference. Moreover, the Eu-CDs can be successfully utilised to detect the content of tetracycline in eggs and fish using the standard addition method, respectively. Finally, the mechanism of ratio fluorescence detection of tetracycline has been discussed in detail. Meanwhile, the Eu-CDs can be used for the determination of tetracycline in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eu-CDs
Ratio fluorescence probe
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence analysis of tetracycline in DMF
Detection of tetracycline in real samples
Morphology and optical properties of the Eu-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of preparation Eu-CDs probe for detecting detected tetracycline.
(A) TEM and size distribution (inset) images of Eu-CDs. (B) EDS spectrum of Eu-CQDs. (C) FT-IR spectra of Eu-CDs. (D) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Eu-CDs.
(A) XPS survey spectra of Eu-CDs. (B) XPS C1s spectra of Eu-CDs. (C) XPS O1s spectra of Eu-CDs. (D) XPS Eu3d spectra of Eu-CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectrum with different excitation wavelength of Eu-CDs (340–410 nm). (B) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs at different pH. (C) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs with various concentrations of NaCl. (D) Fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs at different time.

(A) Selectivity analyses of fluorescent probe Eu-CDs and mixed various interfering chemicals, respectively; (B) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-CDs in the presence of increasing tetracycline concentrations (0–80 μM) in DMF; (C) The relationship between I623/I473 and tetracycline from 0.1 to 80.0 μM.
(A) Excitation and emission spectra of the Eu-CDs and UV–vis absorption of tetracycline. (B) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of Eu-CDs in the presence and absence of tetracycline.
The comparison of Eu-CDs with other reported for tetracycline detection.
Recovery test of tetracycline in egg and fish samples.

head.ce:title: A ratio fluorescence probe by one-stage process for selectivity detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang Hunan Province, 421001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of South China, Hengyang Hunan Province, 421001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the rapid development of society, drug residues in people's living environments are becoming increasingly serious. When the drug residues in the human body exceed a certain standard, it will have a number of adverse effects on human health. Therefore, a ratio fluorescent probe (Eu-CDs) was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method for the selectivity detection of tetracycline. The ratio of fluorescence intensity (I623/I473) and tetracycline concentration demonstrated a good linear relationship that ranges from 0.1 to 80 μM with a low detection limit of 0.057 μM in DMF (N,N-dimethylformamide). Similarly, the fluorescent probe presents good selectivity as well as anti-interference. Moreover, the Eu-CDs can be successfully utilised to detect the content of tetracycline in eggs and fish using the standard addition method, respectively. Finally, the mechanism of ratio fluorescence detection of tetracycline has been discussed in detail. Meanwhile, the Eu-CDs can be used for the determination of tetracycline in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: With the progress of human society and the rapid development of science and technology, numerous negative effects brought by human activities are gradually becoming apparent [1]. At the top of the ecological chain, the problem of drug residues in the environment and animals has steadily attracted people's widespread attention. The widespread use of antibiotics has led to an increase in antibiotic-resistant antibiotic residues in human living environments, endangering humans through the food chain. Excessive drug residues have the following negative consequences on the human body: effects on the digestive tract, liver damage, and kidney damage, impact on tooth and bone development, allergic reaction, imbalance of intestinal flora, along with the formation of a complex that hinders utilisation [2,3].

Tetracycline antibiotics belong to broad-spectrum antibiotics. If they are present in water for an extended period of time, they are easy to cause harm to the ecological environment in the environmental water and even result in the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria, irreversible damage to the ecological structure, and serious pollution to living water [4,5]. Tetracycline antibiotics in environmental water are widely sourced, including environmental microorganisms, medical wastewater, veterinary antibiotics, livestock and poultry manure agricultural and sewage treatment sources [6,7], and so on. In order to ensure food security and protect consumer health, several countries have defined the allowable residual amount of tetracycline in animal-based foods. For example, the European Union and U.S. Food and Drug Administration specified the allowable residual amount of 225 nM and 676 nM tetracycline in milk [8]. It will cause allergic reactions, central nervous reactions, and other adverse reactions when taken improperly or used for a longer time. UV–Vis spectroscopy [9], high-performance liquid chromatography [10,11], capillary electrophoresis [12], electrochemiluminescence analysis [13], and chemiluminescence [14] are currently used to detect antibiotic residues. However, these methods have a number of disadvantages such as complex operation, low sensitivity, narrow dynamic range, and cumbersome operation. Hence, the development of a rapid, accurate, sensitive, and efficient analysis method for the detection of antibiotics in environmental water is crucial in monitoring the level of antibiotic residues in environmental water, as well as improving water pollution and reducing antibiotic residues.

In recent years, new fluorescent detection methods based on fluorescent probes [15] have attracted wide attention owing to their advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, rapidness, ease of operation, and lower cost. A variety of fluorescent probes have been reported for detecting tetracycline, which consist of quantum dots [2,16], molecularly imprinted fluorescence sensors [17], and metal coordination polymers [18]. These probes usually detect a target by varying the intensity of a single fluorescence signal. Environmental and human determinants frequently affect the accuracy of these methods. In order to overcome these problems, some ratio fluorescence probes have been proposed for sensitive and accurate detection of target objects. For instance, Zhou et al. [19] designed SiQDs-Eu3+ ratio fluorescent probes for the purpose of detecting tetracycline based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer, although the process involves the preparation of carbon dots and the addition of europium nitrate in proportion, the detection process was complicated. Shi et al. [20] prepared MoOx QDs ratio fluorescent probes and employed them for the detection of levofloxacin through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photo-induced electron transfer (PET). The ratio fluorescence probe preparation process is difficult and insensitive, despite the fact that these methods can reduce some environmental interference factors.

In this work, a ratio fluorescence probe of Eu-CDs was prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and europium nitrate in DMF, which was used for detecting tetracycline. The Eu-CDs were applied for ratio fluorescence detection of tetracycline without any surface modification. The fluorescence of Eu-CDs was gradually suppressed at 473 nm as tetracycline was added, but the fluorescence of Eu- tetracycline increased continuously at 623 nm. The detection system indicates an obvious colour change from blue to red as the concentration of tetracycline increases under UV light (Fig. 1 ). Due to the overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of Eu-CDs and the UV–visible absorption spectrum of tetracycline, and the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-CDs only changes little after adding tetracycline, there is an internal filtration effect (IFE) between tetracycline and Eu-CDs. The experimental results reveal that Eu-CDs have relatively high selectivity, a wide linear range, as well as a low detection limit for detecting tetracycline. Eu-CDs were found to be practical for detecting tetracycline in eggs and fish samples, demonstrating the advantages of sensitivity, selectivity, and resistance to environmental interference.

Citric acid (>99%), europium nitrate (>99%), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), oxytetracycline hydrochloride (>95%) (OTC), chlortetracycline hydrochloride (>95%) (CTC), gentamycin sulfate (>95%) (GEN), doxycycline hyclate (>98%) (DOX), kanamycin sulfate (>94%) (KAN), levofloxacin (>98%) (LEV), amoxicillin trihydrate (>98%) (AMO), metronidazole (>99%) (MET), sulfadiazine (>98%) (SUL), were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China, https://www.aladdin-e.com/).

The fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4700, Hitach, Tokyo, Japan), and the excitation voltage was 500 V. The spectra were collected in the range of 400–620 nm with different excitation.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) in the range of 200–600 nm.
The FTIR spectra were recorded by a FTIR Spectrometer (Nicolet iS 5, Thermo, American) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1.

The samples for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) were prepared by dropping the Eu-CDs dispersed in methyl alcohol onto an ultra-thin carbon support membrane copper mesh and dried at ambient temperature. The TEM images were collected by a transmission electron microscope (FEI Talos F200s, Thermo, American).

Eu-CDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and europium nitrate in Teflon reactor. 1.2 g citric acid and 0.042 g europium nitrate were dissolved in 25 mL of DMF. Then the precursor was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon reactor and keep warm for 4 h at 170 °C. After cooled down to room temperature, the DMF and the unreacted matters were removed by dialysis with ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 3500 Da). Finally, Eu-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of tetracycline in DMF
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis of tetracycline were performed under the optimal conditions that 10 μL of Eu-CDs solution (50 μg mL−1) were firstly dispersed into 1 mL DMF adding various concentrations of tetracycline solution after incubation for 5 min in the dark environment, then the fluorescence intensity was recorded upon excitation at 400 nm with slit widths of 5/5 nm and emission wavelength at 473 nm and 623 nm.

To further investigate the selectivity of the ratio fluorescence method detected tetracycline by Eu-CDs, the effects of common inorganic ions and analogues on the detection were researched. The common inorganic ions included Co2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, the analogues included oxytetracycline, chlortetracycline, gentamycin, doxycycline, kanamycin, levofloxacin, amoxicillin, metronidazole, sulfadiazine, etc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tetracycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Concentrations of tetracycline in egg sample was quantified to evaluate the usefulness of the method. Firstly, 10 mL of 1% trichloroacetic acid was added to 10 g egg whites to precipitate the protein, and the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min after ultrasonically for 20 min. Then, the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove protein, and diluted to a final volume of 100 mL.

Concentrations of tetracycline in fish sample from supermarket was quantified to evaluate the usefulness of the method. 10 mL of PBS (pH = 6.0) was added to 1 g of crushed fish, ultrasonically for 20 min, and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Then, 10 mL of 1% trichloroacetic acid was added to 10 g supernatant to precipitate the protein, and the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min after ultrasonically for 20 min. Then, the protein was removed through 0.22 μm filter membrane, and then diluted to a final volume of 10 mL.
Subsequently, the recovery experiments were spiked with different concentrations (1.0, 10.0 and 20.0 μM) of standard tetracycline solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of the Eu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, citric acid was selected as carbon source and europium nitrate was used as a doping reagent for preparing Eu-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method. As depicted in Fig. 1, the blue fluorescence of Eu-CDs fades away as tetracycline concentration increases in DMF, and the solution gradually showed red fluorescence under 365 nm of an ultraviolet lamp.

The morphology of Eu-CDs was characterized by TEM. As Fig. 2 A indicates that Eu-CDs have no large-size particles and have a uniform size. Eu-CDs particle size distribution range is 1–3 nm, primarily distributed at 1.65 nm. The presence of europium in Eu-CDs was confirmed by an EDS spectrum (Fig. 2B). The above results prove that we have successfully prepared Eu-CDs with uniform particle sizes.

FT-IR is usually used to identify the type of surface functional groups of prepared Eu-CD. As Fig. 2C shows a broad peak at 3500 cm−1 correlating with stretching vibrations of –OH. The band around 2920 and 2846 cm−1 can be attributed to the C–H of the methylene group. The other peaks at 1649 and 1082 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of CO and C–O, respectively [21,22].As can be observed from Fig. 2D that the absorption of Eu-CDs is primarily concentrated in the ultraviolet region, with the absorption peak at around 300 nm, which can be classified as n-π* transition on CO.

According to the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of Eu-CDs (Fig. 3 ), the presence of C, O, and Eu elements in Eu-CDs can be inferred. In order to obtain more detailed information, further analysis of the C1s spectrum of Eu-CDs (Fig. 3B) points out that there are different forms of bonding states of carbon elements in Eu-CDs, which consist of C–C (284.1 eV), C–O (284.8 eV) and CO (285.5 eV) [5] configurations. As Fig. 3C illustrates, the O1s spectrum of Eu-CDs has C–O (530.8 eV) and CO (532.4 eV) [23]. The Eu 3d spectrum of Eu-CDs (Fig. 3D) exhibited the presence of two peaks at 1134.3 eV and 1165.1 eV, which belong to Eu 3d5 and Eu 3d3 [19], respectively. The results of XPS spectrum analysis are consistent with those of FT-IR.

For the purpose of studying the optical properties of Eu-CDs, the effects of different excitation wavelengths on the fluorescence properties of Eu-CDs were investigated. Fig. 4 A shows the fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs at various excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4A, in the excitation wavelength range of 340–410 nm, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength of Eu-CDs not only changes in fluorescence intensity (first increases and then decreases) but likewise changes in position (red shift). This indicates that the fluorescence spectra of prepared Eu-CDs had a certain excitation wavelength-dependent effect, which could be related to the surface state of Eu-CDs.

One of the reference indexes of the optical performance of Eu-CDs is optical stability. We analysed the change of fluorescence intensity of Eu-CDs at different pH, ion strength, and placement times. As presented in Fig. 4B, the F/F0 of Eu-CDs was comparatively stable with different pH from 4 to 10. Furthermore, the F/F0 of Eu-CDs was stable with a 0–2.0 M NaCl solution concentration (Fig. 4C). As can be seen from Fig. 4D, the F/F0 of Eu-CDs hardly changed within 30 days of placement time. All the results indicate that the prepared Eu-CDs had good fluorescence stability.

In order to investigate the specificity of Eu-CDs fluorescence probe for tetracycline, the same concentration (100 μmol L−1) of tetracycline, analogues, as well as common coexisting ions was mixed with Eu-CDs to compute I623/I473. As shown in Fig. 5 A, following the addition of tetracycline, the luminescence ratio I623/I473 increased significantly. However, the luminescence ratio I623/I473 hardly changed or changed a little when other interference was added. The experimental data showed that the Eu-CDs could be employed as a highly selective fluorescent probe for the detection of tetracycline.

For obtaining the detection concentration range of Eu-CDs for tetracycline in DMF, we measured the fluorescence spectra of Eu-CDs with different concentrations of tetracycline (Fig. 5B). With the continuous addition of tetracycline, it can be found that the colour of Eu-CDs liquid gradually becomes red fluorescence under 365 nm (Fig. 1). The luminescence ratio of I623/I473 displays a linear relationship with tetracycline concentration in the range of 0.1–80 μM μM (Fig. 5C). The linear regression equation is Y = 0.02119 × X+0.002453 (R2 = 0.994), where X is the concentration of tetracycline, and the luminescence ratio I623/I473 has a good linear relationship with the concentration of tetracycline. As LOD = 3σ/K, the detection limit is 0.057 μM (σ is the standard deviation of the sample without ions, and K is the slope of the curve between fluorescence intensity difference and tetracycline concentration). To sum up, our detection methods meet the national detection needs. The outcomes revealed that the Eu-CDs can be utilised as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe for detecting tetracycline. From Table 1 it is obvious that the ratio fluorescence probe of Eu-CDs determination of tetracycline possesses a lower LOD in comparison to those reported methods.

We further studied the detection mechanism of the Eu-CDs. As exhibited in Fig. 6 A, the absorption spectrum of the tetracycline and the fluorescence excitation or emission spectrum of the Eu-CDs have a considerable degree of spectral overlap, indicating that there was an internal filtration effect (IFE) [27,28]. For further studying the fluorescence detection mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of Eu-CDs was detected in the presence and absence of tetracycline. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence lifespan of Eu-CDs was 2.05 ns, with just a little change to 2.64 ns following the addition of tetracycline, so there is an IFE between tetracycline and Eu-CDs.

In order to further test the practicality of Eu-CDs obtained, eggs and fish were selected as real samples from the market for the practicality test, and the results are summarised in Table 2 . Recoveries of tetracycline in the eggs and fish samples were in the range of 97.5%–102.9% with an RSD of less than 2.4% (n = 3). All of these findings reveal that the ratiometric fluorescent probe of Eu-CDs shows great accuracy as well as applicability in the analysis of real water samples.

A ratio fluorescence detection method was employed to detect tetracycline with good selectivity and exhibited good sensitivity detection for tetracycline with a detection limit of 0.057 μM. The Eu-CDs ratio fluorescent probe has the advantages of easy synthesis, inexpensive, high selectivity, stable structure, and no environmental interference. We found the ratio fluorescence of detection for tetracycline via IFE mechanisms. The Eu-CDs fluorescent probe was successfully employed for detecting tetracycline in practical samples.

Sen Liao: Formulation of overarching research goals and aims, Performing the experiments, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jiaxin Liu: Preparation, Performing the experiments. Junjie Luo: Conducting a research and investigation process. Shuo Wang: Preparation, Performing the experiments. Yu Li: Software, Validation. Qingbi Li: Investigation, Data Curation. Shuangshuang Chen: Investigation, Supervision. Guoping Wang: Writing- Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Scientific Research Foundation of University of South China [190XQD104] in China, the Hunan Province Education Department General Project [20C1609], the Program of Innovative Research for Undergraduate of University of South China [X202110555353, X202110555356, X202210555059].
